planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp coreopsis grows naturally in moist to seasonally flooded pine flatwoods, wet prairies, and pond margins of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic, sandy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp coreopsis is the wetland-adapted coreopsis of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, growing naturally in moist pine flatwoods, wet prairies, and seasonal pond margins where most other coreopsis would drown. It produces the classic bright yellow, daisy-like coreopsis flowers from summer into fall on stiffly erect, branching stems. Unlike the drought-tolerant species commonly sold in garden centers, this species requires consistent moisture and even tolerates seasonal flooding. An excellent choice for rain gardens, moist meadow plantings, and boggy areas where it blooms prolifically through the summer months. Provides nectar for swallowtail butterflies, native bees, and goldenrod soldier beetles. Seeds are consumed by goldfinches and other small birds in fall and winter. Highly appropriate for zone 9a wet-site plantings.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; fringeleaf coreopsis grows naturally in moist, open woodlands and woodland edges in the southeastern Coastal Plain and blooms in fall — one of the latest-blooming native coreopsis species.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich or loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; fringeleaf coreopsis is a fall-blooming native of the southeastern Coastal Plain, extending its bloom season later into the autumn than most coreopsis — a significant advantage in the garden where its bright yellow flowers provide critical fall color and nectar from September through November. The leaves are somewhat different from other coreopsis — broader, almost rounded, with slightly fringed or undulate margins that give the species its common name. It grows naturally in moist, open woodlands, forest edges, and the transition zones between flatwoods and upland habitats, tolerating partial shade better than sun-demanding coreopsis species. The extended fall blooming is exceptionally valuable for migrating monarch butterflies and late-season pollinators. Well-adapted to zone 9a conditions in moist, partially shaded garden situations.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in wet to moist, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun; pine barrens coreopsis grows in wet pine savannas, pitcher plant bogs, and seasonal wetlands of the Gulf Coastal Plain and is one of the few coreopsis adapted to waterlogged, nutrient-poor conditions.

directions: Plant in wet to moist, strongly acidic, nutrient-poor, sandy or peaty soil in full sun; pine barrens coreopsis is a specialist of wet pine savannas, boggy pine flatwoods, and pitcher plant bogs of the Gulf Coastal Plain — one of the biologically richest and most threatened habitats in North America. Unlike the drought-tolerant cultivated coreopsis of garden centers, this species grows in consistently wet, nutrient-poor, acidic conditions typical of longleaf pine bog communities. The delicate, almost leafless stems bear a single, large, bright pink to lavender-rose flower — a striking departure from the yellow of most coreopsis — making it one of the most ornamentally distinctive species in the genus. The pink flowers are produced in spring and early summer, attracting native bees. This is a relatively rare plant outside of its bog habitats, and growing it from specialty nursery sources supports conservation awareness of longleaf pine ecosystem species.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, slightly acidic, well-drained sandy or rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; greater coreopsis grows naturally in dry, open woodlands, woodland edges, and sandy barrens across the southeastern Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in dry, lean, slightly acidic, well-drained sandy or rocky soil in partial shade to full sun; greater coreopsis is a tall, robust native perennial of dry, open woodlands and woodland edges across the southeastern United States — a larger, more woodland-adapted alternative to the familiar lanceleaf and threadleaf coreopsis of full-sun meadow gardens. The plant produces bright yellow coreopsis flowers on tall, branching stems in summer, but distinguishes itself by thriving in the partial shade and dry, lean soil conditions of open forest understories where sun-demanding coreopsis decline. The paired (opposite) leaves often consist of three narrow, spreading lobes that can give the appearance of six leaves at each node. Excellent at the woodland edge where it bridges the gap between sun-garden coreopsis and shade-garden plants, blooming prolifically with just a few hours of direct sun. Seeds provide food for native birds in fall and winter.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in moist to wet, average to rich soil in full sun to partial shade; bearded beggarticks self-sows prolifically and will naturalize in moist, sunny to partially shaded disturbed areas. In moist conditions, it can become a vigorous self-sowing annual requiring management.

directions: Direct sow in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; bearded beggarticks is a spectacular fall-blooming annual native of moist meadows, streambanks, and disturbed wet areas across the eastern United States. When planted in masses, the plants produce an outstanding, richly colored display of large, golden-yellow, daisy-like flowers with dark disk centers that rivals any cultivated annual in visual impact — and provides dramatic masses of color in the fall landscape when many ornamentals have finished. Named for the narrow, awl-like bristles (aristae) on the seed heads, these barbed seeds disperse by clinging to fur and clothing. The flowers are outstanding nectar sources for migrating monarchs, painted ladies, and sulphur butterflies. Self-sows prolifically in moist conditions, so establish colonies where the annual cycle can continue without intervention. Excellent for moist meadow plantings and naturalized streamside areas.
